title: Carls Funeral

jokes.text: You broke into his cabin and had sex while he and his wife were in the next room. Oh... That's Bob.
jokes.setup: Johnny claims he doesn't remember Bob, prompting Roland to jog his memory.
jokes.punchline: The memory jog is a mortifying sexual overshare, and Johnny's casual 'That's Bob' reveals he only remembers people by embarrassing incidents.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of the memory ('in the next room') makes it deliciously uncomfortable, and Johnny's resigned acceptance ('That's Bob') is pitch-perfect character comedy — rich people doing embarrassing things and treating them as minor footnotes. The structure is tight: misdirection then instant deflation. Earns its high score.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Remember when Bob and I went to that conference in Atlantic City? We shared a hotel room, and one night after a few drinks, things got a little... heated.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I don't think—

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: No, no, let me finish. We ended up in the hot tub together, and Bob said something so funny I nearly drowned laughing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: What? It's a beautiful memory!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's not helpful, Roland. That's not helpful at all.

jokes.text: I don't know Carl. We barely know Bob.
jokes.setup: Carl thought highly of Johnny, who is being asked to be a pallbearer.
jokes.punchline: Johnny flat-out admits he barely knows the family at all, undercutting the emotional gravity of the request.
jokes.explanation: Efficient, honest, and very Johnny Rose. The two-sentence combo lands because it keeps escalating the social awkwardness downward. Not spectacular but clean and character-true.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Wait, is that how I know you?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: From that story?

jokes.text: And his arms are so weak. Little muscles like corn nuts.
jokes.setup: Johnny is inventing a back injury excuse to avoid being a pallbearer, asking Moira to back him up.
jokes.punchline: Moira 'helps' but volunteers a completely unnecessary and devastating detail about his physical weakness.
jokes.explanation: 'Little muscles like corn nuts' is an exceptional image — bizarre, specific, and perfectly Moira. She's helping while also casually annihilating her husband. The corn nuts simile is genuinely original and highly quotable. Character integration is nearly perfect: only Moira would both torpedo his alibi and deliver it with such poetic cruelty. One of the episode's best lines.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: We need to talk about what happened. Someone's dead.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What? Who?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: It's your mother.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm standing right here, Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I know. Your favorite plant died.

jokes.text: Mm. Yeah. Shouldn't bother Carl.
jokes.setup: Johnny offers the platitude 'life can kick you in the teeth sometimes' in response to Bob's grief.
jokes.punchline: Roland points out this advice is useless to Bob — then follows up with the darkly comic observation that it definitely won't bother the dead man.
jokes.explanation: Compact dark comedy with a clean logic. Roland's blunt practicality cuts through Johnny's empty platitude with a perfectly calibrated dark observation. Economy is excellent — four words do all the work.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Your back is fine. It's your core that's the problem. You have the abdominal strength of a newborn giraffe.

jokes.text: Is there a gun?
jokes.setup: David spots a chaotic family with a crying baby moving in next door at the motel.
jokes.punchline: David's immediate, completely casual inquiry about whether there is a gun nearby — implying suicidal ideation as a comedic response to mild inconvenience.
jokes.explanation: Three words, maximum impact. The casualness is everything — it's not a dramatic cry for help, it's David treating a minor annoyance with hilariously disproportionate despair. Quintessentially David Rose. Economy score of 10 is warranted: you cannot do more with less.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Thank you, Moira. Very helpful. Perhaps we could focus on solving the actual problem at hand rather than discussing snack foods?

jokes.text: Some townie douchebag is moving in next door. They've got a baby. / Is there a gun?
jokes.setup: David looks out the window and sees the chaotic couple arriving next door with a baby
jokes.punchline: Alexis's response to the news of a baby next door is 'Is there a gun?' — a hilariously nihilistic response from someone who clearly has no use for children
jokes.explanation: Three words delivering maximum Alexis character comedy. The joke requires no setup beyond the situation — 'Is there a gun?' as a response to 'they've got a baby' is perfectly calibrated nihilism for a character who has lived a life of pure self-indulgence. Highly quotable and very economical.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, he's not going to care. He's dead.

jokes.text: It's 20 seconds in the microwave not 20 minutes, you tool!
jokes.setup: A bottle or food item has been thrown because it's too hot from the microwave.
jokes.punchline: Bree's profanity-laced specificity ('you tool') signals the chaotic domestic energy of this couple immediately.
jokes.explanation: Functional character establishment joke. The chaos of the couple reads as funny in context but there's no real comedic construction here beyond broad sketch behavior.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: These new neighbors are absolutely insufferable. Their noise levels are inconsistent with any reasonable standard of—

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, those are your family members.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, that was my point.

jokes.text: I'm gonna go stay at Ted's because I've been on enough spring breaks to know how this is gonna end, and it's not... not good.
jokes.setup: The chaotic new neighbors are moving in and Alexis wants out.
jokes.punchline: Alexis casually reveals she has a rich history of exactly this kind of situation — implying a wild, irresponsible past — and treats it as practical wisdom.
jokes.explanation: Good Alexis character work — she parlays her absurd life experience into a coherent (if disturbing) escape strategy. The 'not... not good' trailing off is a nice touch of understatement.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not saying we should kill them, but I'm not *not* saying it either.

jokes.text: Yeah. Like I don't feel good about it.
jokes.setup: David accuses Alexis of abandoning him with the chaotic neighbors.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's barely-apologetic shrug ('like I don't feel good about it') perfectly captures her self-absorbed but not entirely heartless personality.
jokes.explanation: Brief but character-true. Alexis's faint guilt delivered without missing a step is an efficient comic beat.

jokes.text: You know, they say death is just life except you're not here. You're somewhere else, you know? But that's okay because at least you're somewhere, you know? But when does somewhere become there? And when does there become here?
jokes.setup: Bob is asked how he's holding up after his brother's death.
jokes.punchline: Bob attempts a philosophical meditation on death that rapidly becomes circular nonsense, with each iteration more confused than the last.
jokes.explanation: Bob's grief-addled pseudo-philosophy spiraling into word-salad is well-executed. The 'somewhere/there/here' loop is a nice absurdist construction that reads as genuinely grieving-person logic gone off the rails. A bit long, which docks economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not interested in being part of whatever this is.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, where are you going?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Away. Far away. Somewhere without this level of dysfunction.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's not that bad.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Not that bad? This family makes spring break look like a quiet retreat.

jokes.text: Just, just a coffee, please. For here? Yeah.
jokes.setup: Bob has delivered a lengthy, confusing philosophical monologue about death and Johnny needs to navigate it.
jokes.punchline: Johnny cuts through the existential spiral by simply ordering a coffee — the most mundane possible response, delivered to a server who has apparently been waiting.
jokes.explanation: The structural comedy here is the pivot — from Bob's spiral to the banality of a coffee order. Johnny's complete non-engagement with the philosophy is the punchline. Tight and effective.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David! David, wait! You can't just leave me here with these people. David, your calves are like... they're like two angry grapes in a tube sock. David!

jokes.text: Yikes! That's... That hurts today.
jokes.setup: Johnny is pretending to have a bad back to avoid pallbearer duty; Bob mentions Roland told him about the back.
jokes.punchline: Johnny has to physically commit to the lie in front of Bob, hamming up his movements, then adds 'That hurts today' — performing pain for an audience of one.
jokes.explanation: The callback to his fake injury is satisfying, and Eugene Levy's physical comedy is dependable. The joke is in the performance more than the writing.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: You know, when you really think about it, death is just... it's the end of life.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: And life is what happens before death. So death is really just the conclusion of what came before it.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Which means life is essentially the beginning of death. Everything we do is just... preparing for the end.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: But the end is only the end because there's a beginning. Without a beginning, there'd be no end.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: So really, death is just life that's finished being life. And life is just death that hasn't started yet.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: So when you think about it... they're kind of the same thing.

jokes.text: Johnny exaggeratedly grimacing and groaning as he gets up to join Bob, performing his fake bad back.
jokes.setup: Johnny has invented a bad back excuse; Bob mentions Roland told him about it
jokes.punchline: Johnny must now physically commit to the lie in front of the very man he lied to spare — elaborate groaning, 'Yikes! That's... That hurts today.'
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy callback. The joke is that Johnny's lie now traps him into performing it for Bob directly. The physical commitment — 'Ooh, baby. Ooh, wow. Ugh. Yikes!' — is very Eugene Levy. Rewatch bonus because you see it coming and enjoy the trap closing.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I think about dying here sometimes. Like, what if I die in this room? What if this is where it all ends?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Would you like some coffee?

jokes.text: Carl. Yeah, he was a good man. A good man. He admired you, Johnny. And I him. Yeah. And I him.
jokes.setup: Johnny is pretending to have known and admired Carl while clearly having no memory of him.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's hollow affirmations pile up — 'And I him. Yeah. And I him.' — the repetition exposing the emptiness beneath the social performance.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the painfully transparent fakery. 'And I him' repeated twice is a nice touch — Johnny doesn't even bother to vary his empty tribute. Mild but consistent with the episode's throughline.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Ow! My back! Ow, ow, ow! This is excruciating!

jokes.text: I mean, I'm good one on one, but not-not good in a public situation. I clam up pret-pretty good.
jokes.setup: Bob flatters Johnny as 'well-spoken' and 'polished' and asks him to speak at the funeral.
jokes.punchline: Johnny, a formerly wealthy and powerful executive, claims he 'clams up' in public — a self-deprecating lie that is also presumably true in this new small-town context.
jokes.explanation: Functional but not particularly sharp. The stammer performance carries more weight than the writing itself.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I came to express my condolences, but I can't even remember the dead man's name.

jokes.text: They're like really scummy. Like... very skaggy people, who happened to produce a child that hasn't been taken away from them yet.
jokes.setup: Stevie is describing the new guests at the motel to someone (presumably David).
jokes.punchline: The escalating descriptors culminate in the deadpan qualifier 'hasn't been taken away from them yet' — implying it's only a matter of time.
jokes.explanation: Good Stevie deadpan. The 'yet' does a lot of work — it implies inevitable future CPS involvement with total nonchalance. Competent, not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I... I admire Carl as well. He's a very... capable individual. His work ethic is... commendable.

jokes.text: Imagine being related to them. No, I can't. It's too early. — They're my cousins.
jokes.setup: Stevie and David are bonding over how terrible the new guests are.
jokes.punchline: Stevie reveals they're her cousins — immediately after David said he couldn't imagine being related to them.
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection with excellent timing. David's 'it's too early' to imagine it makes the reveal funnier — he was so close to being spared. Structure is sharp: the reveal lands at exactly the right moment. A well-constructed bit.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm not a public speaker. I can't stand up in front of people and just... talk. It makes me incredibly uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Which is why I'm not going to do this keynote address, regardless of how much they're paying me.

jokes.text: The baby's relatively normal looking.
jokes.setup: David has just learned Stevie's cousins are the people he called 'very skaggy' to her face.
jokes.punchline: David's damage-control attempt is hilariously inadequate — 'relatively normal looking' for the baby is the only faint compliment he can muster.
jokes.explanation: The immediate follow-through on the misdirection reveal is perfectly executed. David's attempt to recover by complimenting the baby — and only the baby, and only 'relatively' — is both deeply in character and genuinely funny. 'Relatively normal looking' is a great phrase that implies everyone else in the family is distinctly abnormal. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, look at this couple. I mean, the outfit choices alone are just... tragic. And did you see how he ordered his coffee? Like, we get it, you're indecisive.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And her shoes? Honey, those went out of style in 2009. Which, granted, was only like five years ago in real time, but still.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait... oh my God. Oh my God, no. That's... that's my cousin and his wife.

jokes.text: No, no they're definitely skaggy. My God, they're so skaggy.
jokes.setup: David is trying to walk back his insults now that he knows they're Stevie's cousins.
jokes.punchline: Stevie completely agrees with his original assessment, removing any social tension — she hates them too.
jokes.explanation: The subversion of the expected cringe-apology scene is well done — instead of David having to grovel, Stevie just agrees, deflating the discomfort while also revealing her own relationship with her family. Very Stevie.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: The baby is... fine.

jokes.text: And was that a factor in the cause of death?
jokes.setup: Stevie explains the complicated family dynamic around Carl — her mom's sister was married to him, they broke up before Sean got Bree pregnant.
jokes.punchline: Stevie casually asks whether the family chaos was a contributing factor to Carl's death, treating potential culpability as a neutral conversational point.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's dark pragmatism distilled into one clean line. Asking if the family drama contributed to a death with zero emotional loading is quintessential Stevie deadpan. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, did you see how she was dressed? I mean, the hem on that dress was literally touching the ground.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And did you hear her accent? I couldn't understand a word she was saying.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's my mom.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: Or someone got Bree pregnant. I mean, Bree's not exactly fussy when it comes to introducing men to her vagina.
jokes.setup: Stevie mentions Sean got Bree pregnant.
jokes.punchline: Stevie casually questions paternity and follows up with a bluntly clinical assessment of Bree's sexual promiscuity.
jokes.explanation: 'Introducing men to her vagina' is a funny clinical euphemism. The joke is in the phrasing more than the concept. Stevie's complete detachment from family loyalty makes it work.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, given the number of people your cousin has slept with, I'd say the real question isn't who the father is. It's who it isn't.

jokes.text: Tell me it's not always this clean in here. No, it's-it's always this clean. Oh... it smells so fresh! Uh... that's the Febreeze. I was... I was eating Indian last night, so...
jokes.setup: The Roses are staying somewhere (presumably Ted's clinic or another location) that is notably cleaner than their motel room.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's explanation that the freshness is Febreze masking last night's Indian food is a self-undermining admission — the cleanliness is artificial and the truth is still a bit gross.
jokes.explanation: A functional fish-out-of-water scene beat. Moira's delight in basic cleanliness implicitly mocks their motel situation. The Indian food admission undercuts the nice moment with a mild gross-out, which is a decent structural move.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, I had some questionable sushi for lunch, and let's just say the Febreze is a necessary precaution for my digestive system's inevitable rebellion.

jokes.text: So much better than the motel. At least you know what the smell is.
jokes.setup: The Roses are relieved to be somewhere cleaner than their motel.
jokes.punchline: Moira's wry 'at least you know what the smell is' implies the motel has unidentifiable odors — a devastating indictment delivered with aristocratic calm.
jokes.explanation: Moira's delivery of motel horror through refined understatement is a recurring pleasure. This particular line has good economy and the implication (the motel smell is a mystery) is funnier than any explicit description would be.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'd like you to meet my dogs. This is Schnaps and this is Ginger.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, they're so cute!

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yeah, they're great. Fair warning though, they both have pretty significant parasite issues.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh...

jokes.text: Yeah, your worms are almost all gone, aren't they? Yeah...
jokes.setup: Ted is introducing Macy and Fifer (his dogs) to Alexis and her family at his vet clinic.
jokes.punchline: Ted baby-talks his dog about its nearly-resolved worm infestation in front of company as if this is completely normal and charming.
jokes.explanation: A compact cringe moment — Ted's vet-brain overrides his social brain, treating worm news as cute small talk. Very specific to his character and delivered with full sincerity, which makes it land.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: The dogs are actually very friendly. They just need to get to know you. It's like... they're my children, and you're going to be their new mother.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, eventually. When we're ready to take that step in our relationship.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ted, we've been dating for like three weeks.

jokes.text: Just... for tonight, think of them as... our kids.
jokes.setup: Alexis is clearly uncomfortable around Ted's dogs; Ted tries to make her feel better.
jokes.punchline: Ted suggests thinking of his dogs as 'our kids' — a leap of domestic presumption that is simultaneously sweet and massively misjudged.
jokes.explanation: Good Ted character comedy — his earnest overreach lands because it comes from a place of genuine affection but is tonally completely off. 'Our kids' for a new-ish couple with an animal-averse partner is a nice cringe beat.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: So there I was, trapped into giving a eulogy for someone I barely knew. What was I supposed to do? I couldn't very well stand up there and admit I had no idea who the deceased was.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: So I did what any reasonable person would do. I gave a ten-minute interpretive dance number set to Enya.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Not a dry eye in the house. They thought it was the most moving tribute they'd ever seen.

jokes.text: You take his hands in yours, you look him in the eye, and say, 'no.'
jokes.setup: Johnny explains he agreed to speak at the funeral because Bob was weeping and he didn't know what else to do.
jokes.punchline: Moira demonstrates — with complete specificity — exactly how to say no to a weeping man. The gesture is almost tender in its ruthlessness.
jokes.explanation: The precision of Moira's instruction is the joke — she's not being cruel, she's being practical, but the specificity (take his hands, look him in the eye) makes it feel like she has a tested protocol for this exact situation. It's a line that reveals everything about Moira Rose. Highly quotable and character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Why is the dog staring at us?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Oh, he does that. I think he's just curious.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Curious? Ted, we're in bed together.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, he's a dog. I don't think he really understands the concept of privacy.

jokes.text: What do you say about a guy you couldn't care less about? Oh, it's Gord what's-his-name's funeral all over again. Who's Gord what's-his-name? Exactly.
jokes.setup: Johnny asks how he'll eulogize someone he doesn't know.
jokes.punchline: Moira invokes a precedent ('Gord what's-his-name') and Johnny's question 'who?' is immediately answered by Moira's 'Exactly' — the name itself containing the punchline.
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptionally well-structured micro-bit. 'Gord what's-his-name' as a name implies no one ever knew him well enough to remember his surname. Johnny asking 'who?' and Moira's 'Exactly' is a perfect two-step that needs no further explanation. The structure compresses a lot of character (Moira's famous social world, Johnny's confusion) into four words of payoff. Strong craft.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Is he smelling the... Oh my God, he knows.

jokes.text: I didn't have a thought in my head, so I just stood up and sang 'Danny Boy.' Not a dry eye in the house.
jokes.setup: Moira recounts being cornered into saying words at Gord's funeral with nothing prepared.
jokes.punchline: Her solution — impromptu performance of 'Danny Boy' — worked inexplicably well, positioning her as someone who can replace preparation with theatrical instinct.
jokes.explanation: The Danny Boy solution is both absurdist and perfectly Moira — her theatrical instincts override any social protocol. It also seeds the callback perfectly. 'Not a dry eye' delivered with total sincerity is the ideal Moira punctuation. Rewatch bonus because you know what's coming.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, can you guys just— can you please just get out?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Get out! Out! OUT!

jokes.text: I'm so glad you're not my brother.
jokes.setup: Stevie and David are in a comfortable domestic moment together (on Ted's nice bed, contrasting with the motel).
jokes.punchline: Stevie's non-sequitur expression of relief that David isn't her brother is both endearing and subtly implies she finds the new neighbors' sibling dynamic horrifying.
jokes.explanation: Sweet and funny in the way early Stevie/David scenes often are. The subtext (comparing Sean/Bree chaos to their own dynamic) is implicit and the warmth underneath it lands. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm trying to sleep. Could everyone please be quiet?

dialogue.speaker: Sean
dialogue.line: Sleep? That's just practice for death. Why would you want to practice that?

jokes.text: Hey, the little guy is looking at us.
jokes.setup: David and Stevie are settling in for the night at Ted's place when one of the dogs watches them.
jokes.punchline: David's nervous, uncertain commentary on being watched by a dog signals his deep discomfort and invites the audience to imagine his wide-eyed expression.
jokes.explanation: A mild visual-reaction beat. Functional setup for the dog chaos to come but not particularly strong on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You smell like a brewery had sex with a Christmas tree.

jokes.text: Um... maybe we could invite it to... go? Go away.
jokes.setup: The dog continues to stare at David and Stevie.
jokes.punchline: David attempts to address the dog politely ('invite it to go') — his instinct to be socially gracious even with animals is a funny character tic, stumbling into the actual ask.
jokes.explanation: The halting phrasing is the joke — David's too polite to just shoo the dog, so he tries to diplomatically suggest it leave. Character-consistent and lightly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What was the cause of death?

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, it was... it was autoerotic asphyxiation.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Yeah, he was found... in a very compromising position.

jokes.text: Oh, now you want in on the action, too, huh? Okay, the... the big one is sniffing the diaper. Does... does that mean it's full?
jokes.setup: The dogs are increasingly intruding on David and Stevie's private time, with a second dog now joining.
jokes.punchline: David's panicked, absurd question — is the dog sniffing the diaper to signal it's full? — reveals a man completely overwhelmed by basic animal behavior.
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the dog chaos is well-paced, and 'does that mean it's full?' is a genuinely funny moment of David applying misguided logic to a situation he has no framework for.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, you have to admit, the ceiling fan held up remarkably well.

jokes.text: Out! Out! [followed by] Pass the chips!
jokes.setup: The dog situation has apparently completely derailed any romantic atmosphere.
jokes.punchline: Cut from intimacy attempt to 'Pass the chips' — the dogs have won, and David and Stevie have completely surrendered to a snack-and-TV situation.
jokes.explanation: The structural cut from attempted intimacy to resigned chip-eating is a beautiful comedic pivot. 'Pass the chips' is the perfect punchline for this entire sequence — total defeat, zero drama, maximum funny. The scene transition does the heavy lifting.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, when I'm drunk, I like to ruin my life, so we all have our thing.

jokes.text: Oh, sleep? Well, you know who's sleeping forever, bud? My ex-stepdad, yeah.
jokes.setup: Johnny asks them to keep it down so he can sleep.
jokes.punchline: Sean weaponizes his stepfather's death as a trump card against Johnny's request for quiet, using 'sleeping forever' as a mic drop.
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy that works because Sean is clearly not that broken up about his stepfather — he's just deploying the death rhetorically. 'Sleeping forever' is a nice euphemism that lands as both crass and accidentally poetic.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Is that... a thong?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It was under the bed.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, it didn't get there by itself. And judging by the burn marks, I'd say it was used as a makeshift... what exactly?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: A hair straightener cover.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's disgusting. We're going to have to throw out the entire nightstand.

jokes.text: I hate them, okay? I hate them so much.
jokes.setup: David has clearly been dragged into the neighbors' party and got drunk against his will.
jokes.punchline: David's immediate, emphatic self-defense — 'they got me wasted, I hate them' — while visibly drunk is a relatable and funny capitulation.
jokes.explanation: Functional drunk-David moment. The 'they got me' excuse is familiar but his level of specificity about hating them lands.

jokes.text: You smell... very flammable right now.
jokes.setup: David stumbles in drunk, apologizing.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's observation that David smells 'very flammable' — not 'like alcohol' or 'drunk' but a fire hazard — is a beautifully specific and unexpected word choice.
jokes.explanation: 'Very flammable' is a genuinely original and surprising word choice for 'drunk' — it's both accurate (high-proof alcohol) and absurdly clinical. Only Stevie would describe a drunk friend using a fire safety metric. Economy is near-perfect: five words carry the whole joke. One of the best lines in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Carl was full of life. He was vibrant, he was energetic, he was... he was right here.

jokes.text: Hi, Denim. Hi, sweetie pie.
jokes.setup: David stumbles home drunk and encounters the baby from next door.
jokes.punchline: Both David and Stevie baby-talk 'Denim' — a baby named Denim — without commenting on the name, making the joke the complete normalization of an absurd name.
jokes.explanation: The joke is mostly in the name 'Denim' being treated as unremarkable. The shared baby-talk from both characters adds to the comedy of the moment.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny Rose
dialogue.line: Death is a terrible thing.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny Rose
dialogue.line: Now it's Carl's main characteristic.

jokes.text: Boy, what a beautiful day for a... funeral.
jokes.setup: The Rose family is arriving at Carl's funeral on what is apparently a sunny, pleasant day.
jokes.punchline: Johnny starts to say 'beautiful day for a...' in a reflexive social-positive way, catches himself, and completes it anyway.
jokes.explanation: A familiar joke structure (inappropriate social reflex) but executed with Johnny's particular brand of oblivious politeness. Functional, not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Carl was a man who... who knew how to handle things. He had a real knack for getting deep into situations and really... finishing what he started.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: *whispers* Oh my God.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: *covers face*

jokes.text: Apparently, he was trying to unhook his belt from the ceiling fan and apparently it... It got knotted around his neck.
jokes.setup: Johnny asks how Carl died, framing it as possibly useful for his eulogy.
jokes.punchline: Bob's matter-of-fact description of what is clearly an autoerotic asphyxiation accident — phrased with complete denial ('unhook his belt from the ceiling fan') — is the pivot to one of the episode's darkest jokes.
jokes.explanation: The writing here is genuinely clever — Bob's explanation is technically not a lie, but the audience immediately understands what actually happened while Bob seems to be in denial or deliberate euphemism. The passive 'apparently' doing double duty to signal both uncertainty and avoidance is excellent craft. Dark without being gratuitous.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, at the funeral, the priest kept mentioning how Carl died doing what he loved... which was apparently getting stuck in increasingly smaller spaces.

jokes.text: You'd think the whole thing would've come down, you know, keeping a fan and a body spinning up there for two days. But uh... Carl found the studs on that one.
jokes.setup: Bob is explaining the mechanics of how Carl's body was found — hanging from a ceiling fan for two days.
jokes.punchline: 'Carl found the studs on that one' — Bob pivots from horror to inadvertent home-improvement compliment, praising Carl's structural instincts.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's comedic apex. Bob accidentally eulogizes Carl's DIY competence while describing how his body stayed suspended from a ceiling fan for two days. 'Carl found the studs on that one' is simultaneously horrifying, absurd, and weirdly complimentary — a line that only works because Bob seems to find genuine comfort in it. It's the kind of joke that you can't believe made it to air, and that's exactly what makes it unforgettable. Comparable to the very best dark comedy the show has produced.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm standing here today to say goodbye to someone who was... who was very important. And I keep thinking about mortality, about how we're all just... dust. Aren't we all just dust? I'm dust. You're dust. This podium is dust.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And what's the point? What's any of it for? I spent my entire life building things, acquiring things, and for what? So I can stand here at a funeral and realize it all means nothing?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I don't even know who I am anymore. I came here with nothing. And I'm going to leave with nothing. And somewhere in between, I convinced myself that I was someone important. That I mattered.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And this person we're here to mourn today—they probably felt the same way. They probably lay awake at night wondering if their life had any meaning. And now they're gone. And we're here. And soon we'll be gone too.

jokes.text: Oh... Yeah... That's craftsmanship.
jokes.setup: Bob has just said Carl 'found the studs' on the ceiling fan that held his body for two days.
jokes.punchline: Moira's sincere, beatific 'That's craftsmanship' treats the accidental compliment as a perfectly appropriate tribute to the dead man.
jokes.explanation: Two words. Maximum devastation. Moira's 'That's craftsmanship' is the perfect button on one of the darkest joke sequences in the episode. She's not being ironic — she means it, which makes it funnier. The character integration is impeccable: only Moira Rose, veteran of a thousand social situations, would find the dignified take on 'body dangling from ceiling fan for two days.' Economy score of 10 is warranted. This rivals the 'corn nuts' line as one of the most quotable moments of the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I told you about the time I faked my own death at that gala in Monaco!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You simply announce that the deceased has risen, and everyone leaves immediately out of awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It worked then, it'll work now.

jokes.text: Do you even know what that stain is or... ? Uh... I'm worried if I did, I wouldn't clean it up.
jokes.setup: David is cleaning up after the previous night's chaos in the neighbors' room.
jokes.punchline: David's response — that not knowing the stain's origin is preferable, because knowing would make him stop cleaning — is a beautifully calibrated acknowledgment of willful ignorance.
jokes.explanation: A smart inversion — normally ignorance is presented as bad, but here David has correctly identified that knowing is worse. The logic is airtight and the delivery would be perfectly David. Clean structure.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'm so sorry about the dogs. They've been acting out since we started them on a new food.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: They're actually very ashamed of what happened. They know what they did was wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: We've had several conversations about appropriate boundaries and consent.

jokes.text: Sadly, um, I threw most of the broken glass you see here.
jokes.setup: Stevie asks why David is cleaning up broken glass.
jokes.punchline: David's 'sadly' does the heavy lifting — he's not apologetic about his behavior so much as regretful that he has to clean it.
jokes.explanation: The word 'sadly' is doing exactly the right work here — it's David being self-aware but fundamentally unrepentant. Clean character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, you're a vet? Like a veterinarian?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yes, that's correct.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God. So you see animals... naked?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, animals don't typically wear clothes, so—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's so weird. Don't they feel like, embarrassed? I would be mortified if someone saw me naked like that.

jokes.text: Did I try and make out with you? Mm-mm. Good. But now I'm worried I made out with someone else. Uh, it's kind of my go-to when I'm a little boozy.
jokes.setup: David is piecing together what happened while drunk the night before.
jokes.punchline: The relief that he didn't make out with Stevie is immediately replaced by concern that he made out with someone else — and his admission that drunken makeouts are his 'go-to' is a perfect character reveal.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'good I didn't do it with you' to 'worried I did it with someone else' is a nice comedic structure. 'My go-to when I'm a little boozy' is an endearing and revealing character detail — delivered with David's characteristic breezy self-incrimination.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm just concerned about all these slutty animals running around.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, you know, that's actually a very legitimate concern. We do see a lot of promiscuous behavior in the animal kingdom.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Perhaps we should consider some sort of animal chastity program.

jokes.text: Mm. Mine's shame eating, so yours sounds more fun.
jokes.setup: David reveals that his drunk go-to is making out with people.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's immediate, unguarded self-revelation — 'mine's shame eating' — is delivered with such flatness that it becomes a genuine moment of character intimacy disguised as a joke.
jokes.explanation: 'Mine's shame eating' is a beautiful line — it tells you everything about Stevie's relationship with herself in three words. The contrast with David's outgoing self-destruction (making out with strangers) and Stevie's private self-destruction (shame eating) is a perfect character study compressed into a throwaway comparison. Highly quotable, deeply character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I got you something from a book I was reading.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, is it a Birkin?

jokes.text: Is this your thong or... ? Yeah, no. Bree's a real classy girl.
jokes.setup: David and Stevie are cleaning the motel room after the party.
jokes.punchline: The discovery of a thong, Stevie's confirmation it's not hers, and David's flat 'Bree's a real classy girl' sarcasm lands as a perfect continuation of the 'skaggy' characterization.
jokes.explanation: Functional gross-out sarcasm. 'Real classy girl' is simple irony but the situation makes it land. Slightly cheap but efficiently executed.

dialogue.speaker: Sean
dialogue.line: You know what? I've been thinking about opening my own store.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? What kind of store?

dialogue.speaker: Sean
dialogue.line: I'm gonna call it 'A**hole Emporium.' You know, so people know what they're getting into right away.

jokes.text: Uh, his was wrapped around the remote. Don't worry, I threw out the remote.
jokes.setup: There's apparently also a male undergarment somewhere in the room.
jokes.punchline: Stevie adds the horrifying detail that underwear was wrapped around the remote, and then immediately reassures David she disposed of it — the reassurance making the original detail even worse.
jokes.explanation: 'Don't worry, I threw out the remote' is the right move — the reassurance both escalates the horror and demonstrates Stevie's practical problem-solving. The structure (gross reveal + practical resolution) is clean.

dialogue.speaker: Sean
dialogue.line: Wait... this whole time... my hat's been on backwards.

jokes.text: Well, it looks like they have a side hustle. Uh, I'm pretty sure she strips. Why? Oh no, this is just a lot of um— Yes, it is.— Marijuana.
jokes.setup: David and Stevie are discovering the contents of the neighbors' room.
jokes.punchline: The running inventory of chaos keeps escalating: stripper paraphernalia to large quantities of marijuana, with Stevie's 'And if you're offering, my answer is yes' immediately following.
jokes.explanation: The escalating inventory of chaos is functional comedy — each new discovery is worse than the last. The marijuana reveal followed by Stevie's immediate offer to partake is a nice character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Would you be interested in singing at my cousin's funeral?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I would be delighted to, but isn't your cousin still alive?

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Yes, but we like to book entertainment in advance.

jokes.text: And if you're offering, my answer is yes.
jokes.setup: Stevie and David have discovered a large quantity of marijuana in the neighbors' room.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's flat, immediate acceptance — treating the found marijuana as a casual offering — demonstrates her complete pragmatic opportunism.
jokes.explanation: Short, sharp, and very Stevie. Her willingness to take strangers' marijuana without any moral pause is a perfect character note delivered in maximum economy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A roasted pig? What am I supposed to do with a roasted pig?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's Hawaiian night, Dad. It's a whole thing.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not going to make a pig's ear out of a silk purse.

jokes.text: Thank you for that, Shannon. Nothing says we'll miss you like an excerpt from 'Breaking Dawn,' one of Carl's favourites of the Twilight movies.
jokes.setup: Someone named Shannon has apparently just read from Twilight: Breaking Dawn at Carl's funeral.
jokes.punchline: The minister's dry acknowledgment that a Twilight movie excerpt has been read at a funeral — described as one of Carl's 'favourites' as if this is a normal tribute — is quietly devastating.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of Breaking Dawn — not just Twilight but the fourth and most absurd book — and the minister's utter seriousness in delivering the tribute context makes this land well. It also sets up the bathos of Johnny's upcoming eulogy beautifully.

jokes.text: What to say about Carl... ? Once so full of life and now... lying... very, very close to me.
jokes.setup: Johnny takes the podium to deliver his eulogy for a man he barely knew.
jokes.punchline: His opening observation — Carl, dead in his open coffin, is 'very, very close to me' — is an understated acknowledgment of horror at the physical proximity to the corpse.
jokes.explanation: The uncomfortable physical comedy of standing next to an open casket and noting its proximity is both character-true (Johnny would be thinking this) and structurally clever (it completely derails the expected eulogy tone). 'Very, very close to me' is perfectly understated.

jokes.text: And yet, for Carl, pretty much an all-consuming thing at this point.
jokes.setup: Johnny has said 'what a terrible thing death is.'
jokes.punchline: His qualification — death is certainly 'all-consuming' for Carl specifically at this moment — is an absurd, uncomfortable pivot from platitude to brutal observation.
jokes.explanation: A neat logical joke: death being 'all-consuming' for the dead person lying right there is technically accurate and therefore deeply inappropriate. The structure (platitude → devastating literal application) is clean.

jokes.text: He-he was a- he was a man who loved... A good sweater I'm told.
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to say something specific about Carl but knows nothing about him.
jokes.punchline: 'A good sweater I'm told' — the 'I'm told' immediately reveals he has no personal knowledge and received this detail secondhand at best.
jokes.explanation: 'I'm told' is a two-word stroke of genius. It's the most pathetic and revealing qualifier possible — Johnny can't even confidently claim Carl liked sweaters from personal knowledge. This is a standout line in the eulogy sequence.

jokes.text: And-and he loved the feel of big tool in his hand, am I right, Bob?
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to compliment Carl as a handyman, referencing his DIY skills.
jokes.punchline: The double entendre of 'big tool in his hand' — delivered earnestly and then confirmed with 'am I right, Bob?' — is a classic unintentional innuendo done well, with the appeal to Bob as witness adding to the horror.
jokes.explanation: The double entendre is not subtle, but the earnestness of delivery and Bob's presence as unwilling witness make it work better than it should. Not the most original joke type but executed with commitment.

jokes.text: And I wonder, when Carl was spinning around on that ceiling fan that he was so very proud of...
jokes.setup: Johnny is attempting to make Carl's death sound poetic.
jokes.punchline: Referencing Carl 'spinning around on that ceiling fan' — knowing what we know about how he died — is a callback to the ceiling fan revelation that lands as accidentally graphic.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the ceiling fan death detail, filtered through Johnny's oblivious attempt at poetry, is dark and well-structured. 'Spinning around on that ceiling fan he was so very proud of' combines accidental graphic description with unintentional eulogy absurdism.

jokes.text: Where the hell are you, Sean?! At a bar?! If anyone should be in a bar right now, it's me. Sean!
jokes.setup: Johnny, losing the thread of his eulogy, pivots to confronting Sean (the chaotic neighbor) who is apparently not at the funeral.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's mid-eulogy confrontation of Sean's absence — and his complaint that HE should be in a bar — is both a spectacular breaking of funeral decorum and a genuine character insight.
jokes.explanation: 'If anyone should be in a bar right now, it's me' is a great line — Johnny's self-awareness about how terrible the eulogy is going, combined with his outrage at Sean's absence, creates a genuinely funny outburst. Breaking the funeral decorum completely is a strong comedic escalation.

jokes.text: Well... I-I am here to talk about dying in Schitt's Creek! I'm here to talk about the misery of our lives here and how we might never escape this place, and how we do not wanna end up in a coffin, like Carl, here in this... godforsaken-
jokes.setup: Johnny attempts to recover from his Sean outburst and pivot back to the eulogy.
jokes.punchline: Instead of recovering, Johnny completely abandons the eulogy to deliver a personal confession about his own despair at living in Schitt's Creek — turning a funeral into therapy while the coffin sits beside him.
jokes.explanation: This is where the eulogy reaches its comedic peak. Johnny's total breakdown — pivoting from 'a few words about Carl' to a raw confession about his own fear of dying in Schitt's Creek — is both funny and quietly devastating. The character integration is excellent: this is Johnny Rose's deepest fear given voice at the worst possible moment. The cutoff before 'godforsaken' is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: ♪ Oh, Danny boy, ♪ ♪ the pipes, the pipes are calling... ♪
jokes.setup: Johnny's eulogy has completely derailed into a personal breakdown about dying in Schitt's Creek.
jokes.punchline: Moira, true to her earlier story, saves the day by launching into 'Danny Boy' — executing exactly what she described doing at Gord's funeral. The callback lands perfectly.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's comedic and structural masterpiece. The Danny Boy callback is perfectly seeded and perfectly executed — the moment Moira starts singing is both the rescue and the punchline of the entire episode. The structure (setup 20 minutes earlier, payoff exactly when needed, escalating via the singing through the credits) is exemplary sitcom craft. It also works on an emotional level, which is characteristic of Schitt's Creek at its best. The highest-scoring structural moment in the episode.

jokes.text: So... I'm sorry if, earlier, I wasn't sensitive to your animal issues.
jokes.setup: Ted is apologizing to Alexis for the dog chaos that ruined their evening.
jokes.punchline: 'Your animal issues' as a clinical descriptor of Alexis's fear of/discomfort with dogs is a funny Ted-ism — he's a vet talking about a girlfriend's emotional response to animals in diagnostic terms.
jokes.explanation: A mild but character-consistent Ted moment. Using clinical language for a personal apology is very on-brand. Not a big joke but earns its place.

jokes.text: And if it's any consolation, the dogs told me to tell you that they're super embarrassed about their behaviour, too.
jokes.setup: Ted is apologizing for his dogs' behavior that ruined the evening.
jokes.punchline: Ted claims to be relaying an apology on behalf of the dogs — fully committing to the bit as if this is a reasonable thing to say.
jokes.explanation: Ted ventriloquizing an apology from his dogs is an absurd but character-perfect move. His total sincerity in delivering it is what makes it work — he's not winking at Alexis, he means it.

jokes.text: Well, I am dating a vet. I should've known that... There'd be slutty little animals running around. And I only take in the slutty ones, so...
jokes.setup: Alexis is processing why the evening went the way it did.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's use of 'slutty' for the animals — and Ted's cheerful confirmation that yes, he specifically takes in the slutty ones — is both absurd and a great reversal.
jokes.explanation: 'Slutty little animals' is Alexis's vocabulary applied to veterinary situations, which is a perfect character mash-up. Ted confirming that yes, he specifically takes in the slutty ones is the ideal straight-man reversal — he's not correcting her word choice, he's embracing it as accurate. Warm and funny.

jokes.text: 'Opening your heart to animals: A guide to the benefits of caring for something other than yourself.' It's a very specific book.
jokes.setup: Ted gives Alexis a gift to help her become more comfortable around animals.
jokes.punchline: The book's subtitle — 'caring for something other than yourself' — is a precise and hilarious dig at Alexis's self-absorption, and 'it's a very specific book' is Alexis's own acknowledgment that the message was received.
jokes.explanation: The book title is a masterclass of passive aggressive gift-giving, and 'it's a very specific book' as Alexis's self-aware response rather than an offended one is perfect character writing. Ted's earnestness makes it charming rather than cruel.

jokes.text: Is it a credit card? No. It's a room key... To the premium king suite at the Holiday Inn Express in Elmdale, and they have a very strict no pets allowed policy.
jokes.setup: Ted told Alexis to check out page 37 of the book, which she finds is actually a hidden room key.
jokes.punchline: The reveal that it's a hotel room key — to the Holiday Inn Express (hilariously modest) — with the specific selling point being 'no pets allowed' makes this a perfectly calibrated romantic gesture.
jokes.explanation: The 'Holiday Inn Express' specificity is doing real comedic work — it's romantic enough to be a sweet gesture but modest enough to be funny. 'Very strict no pets allowed policy' as the selling point of a romantic hotel is a perfect inversion of normal romantic-gesture logic.

jokes.text: Thank you. No. Thank you. No. Thank you! Thank you. Thank you! Thank you.
jokes.setup: Ted and Alexis are in a warm moment after the hotel key reveal.
jokes.punchline: The 'thank you' exchange escalates into a competitive loop that parodies rom-com mutual appreciation, going on past the point of comfort.
jokes.explanation: The escalating thank-you exchange is a familiar enough bit that loses some points for originality, but it works as a character beat for Ted and Alexis's dynamic. Slightly too long to be tight comedy.

jokes.text: You know where I got my hat, brah? Where? 'Assholes' R 'Us'.
jokes.setup: Sean asks David about his hat in what appears to be a confrontational tone.
jokes.punchline: David's comeback — 'Assholes' R 'Us'' — is a sharp, preemptive insult that ends the exchange before it can escalate.
jokes.explanation: 'Assholes' R 'Us'' is a punchy, quotable comeback. The Toys 'R' Us parody is familiar but used well here — it's fast, dismissive, and very David. Economy is high.

jokes.text: Oh! Ow! Oh my God. We need to go. We need to go! We need to go!
jokes.setup: Something physical happens at the motel/neighbors' area that causes pain and panic.
jokes.punchline: The rapid escalation from 'Ow' to full flight panic is characteristic Johnny physical comedy — he goes from zero to total retreat instantly.
jokes.explanation: Hard to evaluate from transcript alone — this is primarily physical comedy. The escalation from injury to panic reads as funny in principle but without visual context is weaker on paper.

jokes.text: My hat.. I'm wearing it. I'm wearing it...
jokes.setup: There has been some kind of altercation involving David's hat — he appeared to panic that it was lost or stolen.
jokes.punchline: David discovers in relief that the hat was on his head the entire time — the anticlimactic resolution to what appeared to be a crisis.
jokes.explanation: The anticlimactic hat discovery is a functional physical comedy beat — the relief in David's voice at discovering the hat was on him the whole time suggests the comedy is in his delivery. 'I'm wearing it... I'm wearing it...' trailing off in sheepish relief is a good beat.

jokes.text: So I'm thinking to myself, 'Oh, God, what if I die here?' Mm. That was not lost on anyone.
jokes.setup: Johnny is reflecting on his experience giving the eulogy, where he confessed his fear of dying in Schitt's Creek.
jokes.punchline: Moira's dry 'That was not lost on anyone' confirms that yes, everyone in the room understood exactly what Johnny was saying during the eulogy.
jokes.explanation: Moira's confirmation that the room understood Johnny's very public breakdown is a clean callback. 'That was not lost on anyone' is delivered with perfect Moira dryness — acknowledging the disaster without amplifying it.

jokes.text: Uh, let's pilfer a few sandwiches and make a gracious French exit.
jokes.setup: After the disastrous eulogy, the Roses need to leave the funeral reception.
jokes.punchline: Moira's solution — steal some food and execute a 'gracious French exit' — is a perfect Moira Rose approach: entitled (taking sandwiches), self-deluding ('gracious'), and sophisticated-sounding ('French exit').
jokes.explanation: 'Pilfer a few sandwiches and make a gracious French exit' is a beautifully constructed Moira line. The combination of petty theft with a claim to grace, the specificity of 'sandwiches,' and the elevated 'French exit' phrasing all compress a lot of character into very few words. One of the most quotable lines in the episode.

jokes.text: I'd really like you to sing at my cousin's funeral. She's not dead, but... She's been coughing a lot lately.
jokes.setup: A funeral guest compliments Moira's Danny Boy and makes a request.
jokes.punchline: 'She's not dead, but... She's been coughing a lot lately' — the hesitation before the qualifier, followed by the ominous but mundane explanation, is a perfect dark comedy beat.
jokes.explanation: A sharp little dark comedy gem. The structure — anticipatory qualifier, dark pause, anticlimactic explanation — is well-executed. 'She's been coughing a lot lately' as justification for pre-booking a funeral singer is wonderfully morbid and small-town specific.

jokes.text: Take your time, honey.
jokes.setup: Moira has been asked to sing Danny Boy one more time as they close the coffin, and is preparing dramatically.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's resigned 'Take your time, honey' — knowing they were trying to make a gracious exit — is a perfectly sardonic note of defeat.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's quiet surrender to Moira's theatrical instincts is a warm, character-true beat. Not a big joke but lands cleanly.

jokes.text: So, who's pig do we have to slaughter to get an invite?
jokes.setup: Roland is pitching the Hawaiian night pig roast to Johnny.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's question — 'whose pig do we have to slaughter' — adapts the idiom 'whose baby do we have to kiss' into an absurdly literal pig-centric version for the context.
jokes.explanation: A clean tag-joke to end the episode. The wordplay on 'slaughter' in the context of an actual pig roast is light but effective. Good economy for an episode button.

tmdb_overview: Johnny reluctantly agrees to speak at the funeral of a local man he doesn’t really know. Alexis struggles with her new boyfriend’s love of animals.
insight: Character comedy dominates an 81-point episode built on funeral-setting cringe and social awkwardness.
